preFix.title: Index Thumb-Cut Tabs
preFix.subtitle: Cutting and finishing stepped foredge tabs for quick section access in reference books
preFix.excerpt: Thumb-cut index tabs are stepped notches cut into the foredge of a book block, one notch per indexed section, so a reader can open directly to any section without using the contents page. This technique covers marking out, cutting, and colouring the tabs.
preFix.sourceNotes: Reference book structure and thumb-cut technique based on Cockerell, Douglas. Bookbinding and the Care of Books. London: Pitman, 1901.

attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing mat. Hold the metal ruler firmly with the non-cutting hand behind the line of cut, fingers at least two finger-widths back from the blade's path.
attrs.heading: Sharp tools

content.text: Index
thumb cuts
transform a plain notebook or reference book into a quickly navigable object. Each
index section
gets one notch, stepped slightly lower than the one above, so the
text-block foredge
reads as a descending staircase of coloured tabs.
Planning the layout

Before cutting, plan the tab positions. Divide the total foredge height by the number of sections. For a 200 mm foredge height and 8 sections, each tab zone is 25 mm tall. Mark the zone boundaries lightly in pencil on the foredge of the closed, clamped book.

Each tab notch is cut only through the pages that belong to sections above it, so that the notch descends by one step with each section. Section 1 (top) needs no notch. Section 2 gets a notch cut through the pages of section 1. Section 3 gets a notch cut through sections 1 and 2. And so on.
Step 1: Clamp the text block

Place the closed book in a book press or between two boards clamped firmly, with the foredge flush and projecting about 5 mm beyond the edge of the press boards. The book must not move during cutting.
Step 2: Mark the notch positions
Use a pencil and ruler to mark the top and bottom of each notch on the foredge. The notch depth (into the foredge) should be 8 to 12 mm. Mark a consistent depth line across all notch positions.
Step 3: Cut the notches

Use a fresh, sharp craft knife blade with the metal ruler as a guide. Cut straight down through the right pages at the top of each notch, along the depth line (at a right angle to the foredge), then back up at the bottom. Rectangular notches are most common. Semicircular notches need a curved cut with a scalpel and a circular template.
Cut with multiple light passes rather than a single heavy cut, particularly through many pages. A single heavy cut skews through the block and produces an angled tab face.
Step 4: Clean the cut faces

Examine each cut tab face. Any torn paper fibres along the cut can be pressed flat with the bone folder, then re-cut with a fresh blade if necessary. The tab face should be clean and perpendicular to the foredge.
Step 5: Colour the tab faces

Each tab face can be coloured with ink, watercolour, or painted with diluted acrylic to make the sections visually distinct. Work with the book still clamped so the pages cannot splay. Apply colour to the tab face (the stepped foredge that is now exposed), allow to dry, and add a label or initial to identify the section.
Self-adhesive printed labels cut to the tab size are the quickest finish for a hand-made index.
